courses.title: Machine Design I
courses.summary: From stress analysis to sized hardware: static and fatigue design methodology, shafts, keys, fasteners, and welded joints — the course where strength of materials starts buying parts.
taught_from: MIT OCW 2.72 Elements of Mechanical Design (ocw.mit.edu)

lessons.t: Design methodology and factors of safety
lessons.scope: Load cases, failure modes, and design margins chosen with reasons attached. Codes and standards as encoded experience. The difference between analysis and design stated plainly.
Von Mises and Tresca applied to component sizing. Stress concentrations included honestly. A bracket designed from load to drawing callout.

lessons.t: Fatigue design I: the S–N method
lessons.scope: Endurance limits and the modification factors — surface, size, load, temperature, reliability. Where the knockdowns come from and why they stack. Infinite-life design for steel done properly.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Machine Design; Callister & Rethwisch, Ch. 8

lessons.t: Fatigue design II: mean stress and cumulative damage
lessons.scope: Goodman and Gerber treatments of mean stress. Miner's rule with its known dishonesty acknowledged. Fluctuating-load components assessed end to end.

Combined bending and torsion under fatigue; deflection and critical-speed checks. Shoulders, keyways, and their concentration costs. A pump shaft sized and detailed — the pathway's signature component.
Key sizing by shear and bearing. Splines and interference fits as torque paths. Retaining rings and their groove-stress fine print.
Bolt mechanics: preload, joint stiffness, and the load-sharing triangle. Torque–preload relations and their scatter. Gasketed-joint implications for every flange a plant owns.
Fillet weld sizing under direct and torsional shear. Weld groups treated like bolt groups. Fatigue of welds — why the toe is where cracks start.
Helical spring stress and rate derived; solid height and buckling checks. Fatigue of springs under fluctuating load. Valve and relief springs as working examples.
Torque to raise and lower; efficiency and self-locking conditions derived from the friction analysis. Acme threads and collar friction. Jacks, actuators, and valve stems quantified.

lessons.t: Design project: a driven shaft assembly
lessons.scope: Integrative project: size the shaft, keys, bolts, and welds of a belt-driven assembly against static and fatigue criteria. Calculations documented for review. The pattern of defensible design work.

courses.title: Kinematics & Dynamics of Machinery
courses.summary: Mechanisms in motion: linkages, cams, gear geometry and trains, flywheels, and machine balancing — how rotation becomes useful motion, and where the shaking forces that vibration analysts chase are born.

lessons.scope: Links, joints, and kinematic chains; Grübler's mobility count. Four-bar and slider-crank as the industrial workhorses. Inversions and what each one becomes.
Graphical and analytical position solutions. Velocity by instant centers and by vector loops. Reading mechanism speed ratios without simulation.
Acceleration polygons and the Coriolis component in sliding contacts. Slider-crank acceleration solved completely. The source terms for inertia forces.

Follower motion laws — uniform, harmonic, cycloidal — and their acceleration signatures. Profile synthesis and pressure angle. Why cam wear patterns encode their motion law. Filling-machine cams as the local example.
The involute and why conjugate action matters. Module, pressure angle, and interference. Backlash as both necessity and defect.
Simple, compound, and planetary trains; ratio arithmetic including the tabular planetary method. Gearbox ratios decomposed. The gear-mesh frequencies that will reappear in vibration spectra.
Force transmission through mechanisms; free-body chains under motion. Equivalent offset inertia forces. Bearing loads born from acceleration, not just weight.
Turning-moment diagrams and coefficient of fluctuation. Flywheel sizing from energy variation. Presses and compressors as the plants' flywheel machines.

lessons.t: Balancing of rotating and reciprocating masses
lessons.scope: Single- and multi-plane rotating balance done with vectors. Reciprocating unbalance and why it can't be fully balanced. The kinematic truth behind field balancing jobs.
Centrifugal governors as proportional controllers made of iron. Gyroscopic couples on turbine and vehicle bearings. Legacy topics that still explain modern behavior.

courses.title: Heat Transfer
courses.summary: Conduction, convection, radiation, and the exchangers that trade them — with the transient conduction that casting already used, and the fouling economics every process plant argues about.
taught_from: Çengel & Ghajar, Heat and Mass Transfer: Fundamentals and Applications (McGraw-Hill)

lessons.scope: Conduction, convection, and radiation rate equations side by side. Thermal resistance networks for walls and cylinders. Insulation economics and the critical-radius surprise.
The fin equation derived and solved; efficiency and effectiveness. Finned surfaces as area multipliers on air-cooled equipment. When fins stop paying.

Lumped capacitance with its Biot-number entry ticket, then the semi-infinite solid and one-term chart solutions. Heating and quench times estimated honestly. The physics already met in Chvorinov's rule, now in its own right.
preview: Biot number 0.05 for a quenched pin: is lumped capacitance legitimate, and what does τ = ρVc/hA mean physically?
Why does the surface heat flux into a semi-infinite solid fall as 1/√t?
Roughly how does doubling a plate's thickness change its one-term-model cooling time?

lessons.scope: Boundary layers and the Nusselt–Reynolds–Prandtl grammar. Flat plates, cylinders, and tube banks by correlation. Using correlations without believing them past their range.
Developing versus developed flow; constant-flux and constant-temperature results. The Dittus–Boelter workhorse with its fine print. Heat pickup in real piping runs.
Grashof and Rayleigh numbers; vertical plates and enclosures. Hot cabinets and un-fanned motors in Gulf ambient. Why still summer air is an equipment rating problem.
The boiling curve and critical heat flux respected properly. Film condensation on tubes. The phase-change coefficients that make steam equipment compact.

Shell-and-tube and plate architectures; overall coefficient built from resistances, fouling included. LMTD design and effectiveness-NTU rating methods, each derived, each worked once. Fouling monitoring as the maintenance engineer's stake in this lesson.
preview: Counterflow exchanger: hot stream 150→90 °C, cold 30→70 °C. Compute the LMTD.
Fouling resistance doubles: what happens to U and to the outlet temperatures, qualitatively?
When is effectiveness-NTU the only honest method, and why does LMTD fail there?

lessons.scope: Blackbody laws, emissivity, and view factors in simple geometries. Radiation shields. The furnace and the thermographer's camera share this physics.
Two-surface enclosures and network method basics. Combined convection–radiation surface losses. Estimating bare-pipe losses the way an energy audit does.
Diffusion and convective mass transfer via the heat-transfer analogy. Evaporation and drying estimates. The shared mathematics made explicit.
Rate or size a small exchanger against a duty with fouling margins, then critique the design's failure modes. Calculations packaged for review. The heat-transfer counterpart of the shaft project.

courses.title: Manufacturing Processes III — Sheet, Plastics & Additive
courses.summary: The discrete-products half of manufacturing: sheet-metal working with springback treated properly, plastics processing, packaging operations, and additive — Kirby's panel lines and KDD's filling halls as the anchors.
taught_from: Groover, Fundamentals of Modern Manufacturing (Wiley) — sheet metal & plastics chapters
Kalpakjian & Schmid, Manufacturing Engineering and Technology (Pearson)

lessons.scope: Shearing, blanking, and punching; clearance and its burr signature. Cutting force estimates. Nesting and material yield as quiet profit levers.

Bend allowance from neutral-axis geometry; bending force; and springback — why it happens, how much to expect, and the compensation strategies press brakes actually use. Kirby's roll-formed and brake-formed building panels as the standing example.
lessons.src: Groover, Ch. 20; Kalpakjian & Schmid, sheet-forming chapter
preview: Compute bend allowance for a 90° bend, R = 6 mm, t = 3 mm.
Springback scales with which material ratio — and why does high-strength steel spring back more than mild steel?
V-die bending force: what happens to required tonnage when sheet thickness doubles?

lessons.scope: Drawability and the limiting draw ratio; blankholder force and defect map — wrinkling versus tearing. Redraws and ironing. Cans and cookware as engineered products.

lessons.t: Roll forming and high-volume sheet lines
lessons.scope: Progressive roll forming of profiles; line balancing and tooling. Coil handling, leveling, and slitting upstream. The continuous logic of Kirby-style panel production.
Mechanical and hydraulic presses; die sets and progressive dies. Tonnage curves and where presses are overloaded. Die maintenance as a production-uptime discipline.
Screw extruder anatomy and melt behavior. Die swell and downstream sizing. Cable insulation extrusion — Gulf Cable's polymer line — as the worked context.
The molding cycle: fill, pack, cool, eject. Gate and runner logic; shrinkage and warpage. Defects — shorts, flash, sinks — mapped to their process causes.
Bottles, trays, and films — the packaging trio. Parison control and wall-thickness distribution. Food-grade material and hygiene constraints introduced.

Filling, capping, labeling, date-coding, and case packing as a machine system; line speed, accumulation, and the V-curve logic that protects the filler. Hygienic design and changeover discipline. The KDD-style dairy line read as one machine.
preview: Why is the filler the protected center of a packaging line, and what does the V-curve of speeds around it do?
Name two hygienic-design features that separate food-line equipment from generic machinery.
Why does changeover reduction (SMED) buy more capacity on packaging lines than almost anywhere else?

lessons.scope: PBF, DED, material extrusion, and binder jetting with their materials and defect signatures. Post-processing realities. Where AM fits a maintenance organization: fixtures and unobtainable spares.
DFM/DFA rules that move cost: part-count reduction, self-locating features, tolerance realism. Redesign exercises on real hardware. The conversation between design and the floor formalized.

courses.title: PLC Fundamentals I
courses.summary: The plant's reflexes: PLC hardware, scan cycle, ladder logic, timers and counters, and disciplined I/O wiring — taught to read-and-troubleshoot level first, author level second.

lessons.scope: Contactors, control relays, and hardwired interlocks. Reading a motor-starter schematic fluently. The relay heritage that ladder logic still speaks.

lessons.t: PLC architecture and the scan cycle
lessons.scope: CPU, I/O, rack, and power supply; the read–execute–write scan and its timing consequences. Why outputs update when they do. Watchdogs and fault states.

Contacts, coils, seal-in circuits, and interlocks — the core patterns of industrial logic, each drawn and traced. Start/stop of a motor with safety interlocks built the standard way. Reading unfamiliar ladder cold: the transition engineer's most bankable automation skill.
preview: In a seal-in rung, what keeps the motor contactor energized after Start is released — and what breaks it?
Why are stop functions wired through NC contacts: which failure does that choice survive?
Within one scan cycle, at what point do physical outputs actually change state?

lessons.scope: On-delay, off-delay, and retentive timers; up/down counters. Debounce, staging, and timeout patterns. A star-delta starter sequence programmed and traced.
Sinking/sourcing, wet and dry contacts, and field-device interfacing. Fusing and commons in the I/O rack. The wiring faults behind most 'PLC problems'.
4–20 mA and 0–10 V channels; raw counts to engineering units scaled correctly. Resolution and out-of-range handling. A transmitter read into a displayed value end to end.
Rungs into routines; tags and comments that survive personnel turnover. State-based thinking for sequences. Programs written to be troubleshot at 3 a.m.
Online monitoring, forcing with fear, and cross-referencing. A systematic fault-finding method from symptom to rung to field device. The lesson maintenance hiring managers actually test.

lessons.t: Safety circuits and the PLC's limits
lessons.scope: E-stops, safety relays, and why safety functions live outside standard PLC logic. Categories of safety architecture in outline. The boundary every integrator must respect.
Operator screens: tags, alarms, and navigation that helps at 3 a.m. Alarm philosophy — fewer, truer alarms. The HMI as the machine's public face.
Program a conveyor start-up with permissives, timers, and fault latching; document it; then troubleshoot three injected faults. The full loop: specify, program, verify. PLC II builds from here.

courses.title: Industrial Safety
courses.summary: Staying alive around energy: hazard recognition, permits and isolation, machine guarding, hot work, confined spaces, and the incident lessons the Gulf's process industry paid for in blood.

lessons.t: How people get hurt in plants
lessons.scope: Energy-based hazard thinking: mechanical, electrical, pressure, thermal, chemical, gravity. Incident pyramids and their honest critique. Safety as engineering, not posters.
Hazard identification, likelihood–consequence matrices, and the hierarchy of controls. Job safety analysis written for a real maintenance task. Residual risk stated, not hidden.
Isolation points, stored energy, and verification of zero energy. Group lockout on complex equipment. The procedure that most directly keeps maintenance engineers alive.
Permit types — general, hot work, confined space, line break — and their control logic. Permit interfaces between operations and maintenance. Why permits fail: signatures without verification.
Point-of-operation, nip points, and rotating-shaft hazards. Guard types and interlocked access. The guard-defeat culture problem addressed as engineering.
Definition, atmospheres, and gas testing. Entry roles and rescue planning that isn't fictional. Tanks, pits, and vessels — where multiple-fatality events happen.
Hot-work hazards from welding lecture context; fire triangle and watch requirements. Flammable atmospheres and gas freeing. Fabrication-yard discipline as practiced at scale.
Fall protection systems; scaffolding basics; crane and rigging fundamentals with load charts respected. Dropped-object prevention. The physics of statics applied to staying alive.
Heat illness physiology and the WBGT logic of work-rest cycles. Summer work rules in Kuwait's climate. Hydration and acclimatization as engineering controls, not advice.
Evidence preservation, timelines, and causal analysis without blame-first shortcuts. Corrective actions that change systems, not just people. The investigation skill reused in reliability RCA.
